narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.80—well above the typical 3.21 average—and a competitive LSAT score of 37, slightly edging out the average of 36.70, you've built an impressive academic foundation that many aspire to. Your background also includes an upper-middle-class family income, positioning you comfortably among peers. However, the university's admission model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours from past students, estimated a 54.7% chance that you would fail the bar exam. This tipped the prediction toward failure, leading to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes candidates likely to pass on their first try. The model's decision hinged most strongly on your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which placed you in the bottom tenth of your classmates—far below the average decile of about 5.5—and this low performance strongly pushed the prediction toward expecting bar failure. Next in influence was your strong undergraduate GPA, which actually worked in your favor by pulling the prediction toward bar passage success, counterbalancing some concerns. Your Year 1 decile of 1, again at the bottom of your class compared to the typical 5.7 average, further reinforced the risk of failure, though with somewhat less weight than your later-year grades. While factors like your full-time student status (shared by over 90% of applicants) and other strengths helped, these early and persistent low class rankings overshadowed them in the model's eyes. Compared to typical admitted students, who often rank in the middle or higher deciles throughout undergrad and law school prep, your profile signals higher bar risk despite your standout college record. This doesn't define your potential—many overcome similar early hurdles—but it explains the cautious rejection. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your class rankings, perhaps through targeted tutoring or a post-baccalaureate option, and reapply with that evidence of growth. Gathering letters from professors who can speak to your bar readiness could also strengthen your next application. You're clearly capable; refining these areas could flip the script for future opportunities.
